Part I - Used Car Market Dynamics in Egypt: An Exploratory Analysis¶

Introduction¶

This exploration utilizes a cleaned and combined dataset focusing on the used car market in Egypt. The original data was gathered from two sources: a Kaggle dataset and web-scraped data from Sylndr [4]. The goal of this analysis is to investigate the factors influencing used car prices and identify market trends, focusing on brand dominance, geographic price variations, and model-specific pricing.

Preliminary Wrangling¶

Load dataset and describe its properties through the questions below.

In [17]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
In [94]:
# Importing the required libraries
import pandas as pd

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_style('whitegrid')
# load data
car_df = pd.read_csv('merged_cars_data.csv')

What is the structure of my dataset?¶

The cleaned and combined dataset contains 8912 entries across 9 columns [3]. All columns are non-null and have appropriate data types, including four integers (year, km, cc, price) and five objects (brand, model, color, transmission, city).

What is/are the main feature(s) of interest in your dataset?¶

The main feature of interest is the Price of the vehicle in Egyptian Pounds (EGP), as the underlying problem statement focused on estimating fair market prices. Secondary features of interest include Brand and Model, necessary for determining market dominance.

What features in the dataset do you think will help support your investigation into your feature(s) of interest?¶

Features that will support the investigation into price include Kilometers Driven (km), Year (age/depreciation), Engine CC (cc), and City (location-based price variation).

Univariate Exploration¶

In this section, investigate distributions of individual variables. (Requires at least 15 visualizations total across sections).

Question: What is the distribution of used car prices?¶
In [98]:
car_df_mercedes = car_df.query('brand == "Mercedes"')
car_df_mercedes['price'].max(), car_df_mercedes['price'].min()
Out[98]:
(np.int64(4800000), np.int64(40000))
In [99]:
# -   **Visualization Placeholder (Distribution of Price):** Histogram of the `price` column.
# Histogram of car prices
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(car_df['price'], bins=50, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Distribution of Used Car Prices in Egypt')
plt.xlabel('Price (EGP)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()

# Log-transformed histogram for normalization
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(np.log(car_df['price']), bins=50, color='lightgreen', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Log-Transformed Distribution of Used Car Prices')
plt.xlabel('Log(Price EGP)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()
  • Observation: This visualization would show the central tendency and spread of prices. Given the wide range and presence of luxury cars (e.g., Mercedes prices exceeding 3,000,000 EGP in the raw data), a right-skewed distribution is expected. Logarithmic transformation may be needed for normalization before correlation analysis.
Question: Which car brands are most dominant in the market?¶
In [118]:
top_brands = car_df['brand'].value_counts().head(15)
sns.countplot(data=car_df[car_df['brand'].isin(top_brands.index)],
              x='brand',
              order=top_brands.index,
              palette='viridis')
plt.title('Top 15 Car Brands')
plt.xlabel('Brand')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Top Brands): Bar chart showing the counts of the top 15 values in the brand column.
  • Observation: This plot is crucial for answering the first research question regarding dominant brands. The analysis should confirm the strong presence of Asian manufacturers.
Question: What is the distribution of Kilometers Driven (km)?¶
In [101]:
ax = car_df.hist(column='km', bins=20, figsize=(12, 8))
plt.suptitle('Distribution of Kilometers Driven')
plt.xlabel('Kilometers Driven')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

# Replace x-tick labels with bin range labels
xticks = []
for patch in ax[0][0].patches:
    start = int(patch.get_x())
    end = int(patch.get_x() + patch.get_width())
    xticks.append(f'{start}-{end}')
ax[0][0].set_xticklabels(xticks, rotation=45, ha='right')

plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Distribution of KM): Histogram of the km column.
  • Observation: This reveal that the market is dominated by low-mileage (newer) vehicles.
In [102]:
# Visualization of Top-10 values per column

def visualize_top10(df: pd.DataFrame, exclude_cols: list = None, only: list = None, percentage: bool = False, top:bool = True) -> None:
    """
    Visualize the top-10 (or bottom-10) most frequent values for selected columns in a DataFrame.

    Parameters
    ----------
    df : pd.DataFrame
        The DataFrame to analyze.
    exclude_cols : list, optional
        Columns to exclude from visualization. Ignored if `only` is provided.
    only : list, optional
        If given, only these columns will be visualized.
    percentage : bool, default False
        If True, annotate bars with percentages; otherwise show raw counts.
    top : bool, default True
        If True, plot the 10 most frequent values; if False, plot the 10 least frequent.

    The function creates a vertically stacked set of count plots, one per selected column,
    each displaying the top (or bottom) 10 unique values for that column.
    """
    if only is not None:
        wanted_columns = [col for col in df.columns if col in only]
    else:
        wanted_columns = [col for col in df.columns if col not in (exclude_cols or [])]
    
    # Set up the matplotlib figure
    plt.figure(figsize=(15, len(wanted_columns) * 8))  # Adjust figure size

    # Loop through each categorical column
    for i, column in enumerate(wanted_columns):
        plt.subplot(len(wanted_columns), 1, i + 1)  # Create subplots   
        
        # Get top 10
        if top:
            top_10 = df[column].value_counts().nlargest(10).index
            plt.title(f'Top {len(top_10)} for {column}')
        else:
            top_10 = df[column].value_counts().nsmallest(10).index
            plt.title(f'Bottom {len(top_10)} for {column}')
        ax = sns.countplot(data=df[df[column].isin(top_10)], x=column, order=top_10, palette='Set2')
        # Add count labels on top of bars
        total = len(df[df[column].isin(top_10)])
        for p in ax.patches:
            height = p.get_height()
            label = f'{float(height / total * 100):.2f}%' if percentage else f'{int(height)}'
            ax.annotate(label, 
                        (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height), 
                        ha='center', va='bottom')
        
        plt.xlabel(column)
        plt.xticks(rotation=45)
        plt.ylabel('Count')

    plt.tight_layout()  # Adjust spacing between plots
    plt.show()
In [103]:
# Bottom 10 our car dataframe

visualize_top10(car_df, percentage=True, top=False, exclude_cols=['price','cc','km'])
In [ ]:
# Automatic vs manual
visualize_top10(car_df, percentage=True, top=True, only=['transmission'])

Bivariate Exploration¶

In this section, investigate relationships between pairs of variables.

Question: How does price vary across different cities?¶
In [104]:
# Luxury and expensive used cars where they bought (Model 2020 and byound).

new_model = car_df[car_df['year']>=2020]
new_model_citis = (
    new_model
    .groupby('city')['price']
    .agg(['median','min','max'])
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('median', ascending=False)
    .head(10)
)

new_model_citis
Out[104]:

35 El Senbellawein 900000.0 900000 900000

80 Tagamo3 - New Cairo 757500.0 175000 3600000

In [105]:
sns.boxplot(y='city', x='price', data=new_model[new_model['city'].isin(new_model_citis['city'])], palette='Set3')
plt.title('Price Distribution by City')
# plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Price vs. City): Box plots of price grouped by city for the top 10 cities by volume.
  • Observation: The analysis found that Zamalek and Tagamo3 - New Cairo have noticeably higher price distributions overall, with Zamalek showing the highest median price, and Tagamo3 - New Cairo exhibiting substantial price spread and several high-value outliers.
Question: How is the car price correlated with its manufacturing year?¶
In [106]:
sns.regplot(x='year', y='price', data=car_df, scatter_kws={'alpha':0.5}, line_kws={'color':'red'})
plt.title('Price vs. Year')
# plt.ylim(0, 8000000)
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Price vs. Year): Scatter plot of price against year.
  • Observation: A positive correlation is expected (newer cars cost more).
Question: Does transmission type influence car price?¶
In [107]:
sns.violinplot(x='transmission', y='price', data=car_df)
plt.title('Price vs. Transmission')
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Price vs. Transmission): Violin plots or box plots comparing the distribution of price for ‘Automatic’ versus ‘Manual’ transmission types.
  • Observation:
    • Cars with automatic transmissions generally have much higher price variability, including several very expensive outliers that skew the distribution significantly upward.
    • Manual transmission cars mostly cluster at lower price ranges, with comparatively few expensive outliers and a tighter price spread around the median.
    • The widest section of the violin plot for automatic transmissions is at a higher price level compared to manual transmissions, highlighting where most listings or sales occur for each type.

Multivariate Exploration¶

Create plots of three or more variables to investigate your data even further.

Question: How do Kilometers Driven and Price interact, and is this relationship moderated by Engine CC?¶
In [108]:
car_df.cc.value_counts().head(15).index
Out[108]:
Index([1600, 1500, 1400, 2000, 1300, 1200, 1800, 1000, 3000, 1100, 2500,  800,
       2400, 3500, 3800],
      dtype='int64', name='cc')
In [109]:
data = car_df[car_df['cc'].isin(car_df.cc.value_counts().head(15).index)]
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x='km', y='price', hue='cc', size='cc', alpha=0.5)
plt.title('Price vs. Kilometers Driven (Colored by Engine CC)')
plt.xlabel('Kilometers Driven')
plt.ylabel('Price')
plt.legend(title='Engine CC')
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Price, KM, and CC): Scatter plot of price (Y-axis) versus km (X-axis), with point size or color intensity mapped to Engine_CC.
  • Observation: Price decreases as kilometers increase. Mapping cc provides insight that high-performance (high CC) cars retain value better, especially at lower mileage.
Question: How do the top dominant models vary in price and mileage?¶
In [110]:
frequent_models = car_df['model'].value_counts().head(5)
frequent_models

In [111]:
data = car_df[car_df['model'].isin(frequent_models.index)]
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x='km', y='price', hue='model',alpha=0.75)
plt.title(f'Price vs. Kilometer for Frequent {frequent_models.count()} Car Models')
plt.show()
In [117]:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = car_df[car_df['model'].isin(frequent_models.index)]

# Create FacetGrid for each car model
g = sns.FacetGrid(
    data,
    col="model",
    col_wrap=5,              # Number of columns per row
    height=4,                # Size of each facet
    sharex=False,            # Optionally disable axis sharing
    sharey=True,
    palette="Set2"
)

g.map_dataframe(
    sns.scatterplot,
    x="km",
    y="price",
    alpha=0.75
)

g.fig.suptitle(f'Price vs. Kilometer for Frequent {frequent_models.count()} Car Models', y=1.03)
plt.show()
  • Visualization Placeholder (Top Models, Price, and KM): Faceted scatter plots or overlaid box plots showing the relationship between price and km, separated by the top frequent 5 model entries (e.g., Nissan Sunny, Toyota Corolla, Kia Cerato).
  • Observation:
    • There is a consistent negative correlation between price and kilometers driven for all five car models: as the number of kilometers increases, the price tends to decrease. This confirms the general expectation that higher mileage leads to lower resale values for used cars.​
    • Spread of prices for each model is different at low mileage. Some models (e.g., Cerato, Corolla) show a broader range of prices when kilometers are low, possibly due to variations in year, trim, or condition. Others like Sunny appear more clustered and predictable.
    • A few outlier points (e.g., unusually expensive cars with higher mileage) are visible in some panels. These may represent rare trim levels, recent major maintenance, errors, or over estimations.

Conclusions¶

The exploration successfully investigated market drivers like brand dominance, location-based pricing, and the relationship between age/mileage and price. Nissan Sunny, Toyota Corolla, and Kia Cerato are confirmed as top models. We also confirmed that location significantly impacts maximum pricing, with Tagamo3 - New Cairo exhibiting the highest ceiling [1]
